ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Coconut Chutney. Nearest parent is a coconut-based condiment or relish. A new concept for fresh South Indian coconut chutney is warranted.
ontology.foodex2_description: Condiments and sauces — coconut-based fresh chutney (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of coconut chutney is 2 tablespoons or a small accompanying bowl (approximately 40g) — a smooth, pale-white fresh condiment with a tadka of mustard seeds, red chilli, and curry leaves on top.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut Chutney — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, derived from fresh coconut and chana dal composition data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a standalone coconut chutney entry. Values are derived from ICMR-NIN fresh coconut and roasted chana dal data, blended per standard recipe proportions (grated coconut ~55%, chana dal ~10%, water ~25%, green chilli/ginger/salt/oil ~10%).

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from fresh coconut flesh GI; the high fat and fibre content of fresh coconut gives a very low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 112mg per standard serving (40g) — low; the chutney provides 220mg potassium per 100g which helps offset sodium intake.
hypertension.guidance: Suitable for hypertensive patients at standard serving sizes. Salt can be reduced in home preparation. Fresh coconut provides potassium which is beneficial for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~18); glycaemic load per 40g serving is less than 1 — negligible. High fat and fibre content of coconut slows gastric emptying.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Coconut chutney is an excellent accompaniment for diabetic patients — it adds virtually no glycaemic load to a meal and the coconut fat slows absorption of the accompanying starch.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fresh coconut contains medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) with lauric acid which has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties; green chilli and ginger are anti-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Fresh coconut chutney provides a beneficial lauric acid content and anti-inflammatory spice compounds. Standard serving size is small (40g) so total fat intake is modest.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut Chutney is the indispensable fresh condiment of South Indian cuisine — a smooth, creamy white-beige paste made by grinding freshly grated coconut with roasted chana dal, green chilli, ginger, and salt, then finished with a fragrant tadka (tempering) of coconut oil, mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and curry leaves. Served in a small bowl alongside idli, dosa, vada, and uttapam. It is the cooling, rich, slightly nutty counterpart to the tangy-spicy sambar. Very fresh and perishable — typically consumed on the day of preparation. In Singapore, coconut chutney is available at all South Indian restaurants.

confidence_reason: No standalone coconut chutney entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB Foodo. Values estimated from fresh coconut and chana dal component data per standard recipe proportions. The high fat content from fresh coconut is well-established.
notes: Vegan and gluten-free. Contains tree nut allergen (coconut). Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based preparation. The very high saturated fat per 100g (~15.5g) is entirely from fresh coconut — medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs), primarily lauric acid. At the standard 40g serving size, total fat is approximately 7.4g. Fresh chutney is highly perishable — must be consumed within 1 day.
